---

# Figure Description: Электроразведка (Electrical Prospecting Survey), Чертёж №23, Лист 3

## Figure Type

This is an **index/location map** (карта фактического материала or coverage map) for electrical prospecting (electrometric/resistivity surveys). It shows the spatial distribution and coverage extents of electrical geophysical surveys conducted at different scales across the study area. It is not a results map but rather a **survey coverage diagram** showing where different types of electrical surveys were performed and at what scale.

---

## Coverage and Subject

The figure covers a large portion of the northern Kazakhstan survey area associated with the Западно-Тенизская партия (West-Teniz Party) geophysical expedition, covering work conducted during **1978–1984**. The map is organized into labeled quadrant blocks corresponding to standard Soviet topographic map sheet subdivisions, with the following sheet/block designators visible:

- **XXXIII** (upper central area)
- **XXXIV** (upper right area)
- **XXXV** (far upper right)
- **IV** (central right)
- **V** (right side)
- **II** (left-central)
- **VIII** (lower left)
- **IX** (lower central)
- **X** (lower left margin)
- **XIV** (lower left)
- **XV** (lower central-right)

These likely correspond to subdivisions of 1:200,000 or 1:100,000 map sheets within the M-42 and/or N-42 series.

Numerous numbered contour polygons are distributed across the map, representing individual survey blocks or anomaly contours catalogued by number (e.g., **52, 99, 155, 159, 165, 188, 196, 210, 214, 291, 305, 310, 315, 318, 335, 338, 344, 360, 519, 610, 615, 618, 655, 701**, and others). These numbers correspond to catalogue entries ("номер контура по каталогу" — catalogue contour numbers).

---

## Key Content

The map displays the **areal distribution of electrical prospecting surveys** at multiple scales, distinguished by different border/line styles for each survey block polygon:

1. **Dot-dash border** = surveys at **1:500,000 scale**
2. **Dashed border (medium)** = surveys at **1:200,000 scale**
3. **Dashed border (fine/close)** = surveys at **1:100,000 scale**
4. **Diagonal hatching (solid lines with hatching)** = surveys at **1:50,000 scale**
5. **Single diagonal lines** = **отдельные профили** (individual/isolated profiles)

The survey blocks are irregularly shaped and distributed non-uniformly across the map area, indicating that coverage was carried out in targeted zones of interest rather than as a systematic grid across the entire region. The denser concentration of blocks appears in the **upper-central and central portions** of the map (around sheets XXXIII, XXXIV, II, and IV), with sparser coverage in the **lower and peripheral zones** (XIV, XV, X).

The numbered polygons (catalogue contours) suggest that specific anomalous zones identified during reconnaissance were subsequently targeted for more detailed survey work at larger scales (1:50,000).

There is a **rubber stamp** visible in the right-central area reading:
> **Казахская ССР**
> **Республиканский**
> **геологический фонд**
> **Инв. №36372** (possibly 36572 — partially obscured)

This confirms archival registration with the Kazakh SSR Republican Geological Fund.

---

## Research Value

This figure serves as an **essential metadata and logistics map** for the electrical prospecting component of the survey, showing a geologist or research assistant exactly **where electrical geophysical measurements were taken, at what resolution, and which numbered anomaly zones were investigated**. By cross-referencing the catalogue contour numbers with the accompanying anomaly catalogue, a researcher can quickly identify which specific zones received detailed (1:50,000) electrical survey coverage versus only reconnaissance-level (1:500,000) coverage, directly informing assessments of **data quality and confidence** for subsurface interpretations. In the context of mineral potential evaluation, this map reveals the **spatial prioritization strategy** of the survey team — the clustering of detailed surveys in certain sheet areas suggests those zones were considered most prospective for mineralization or had anomalous geophysical signatures warranting follow-up investigation.
